cards.q: What does pregnancy represent physiologically?
cards.a: A unique physiological state, characterised by profound, reversible adaptations across virtually every organ system in the maternal body.

cards.q: Are the adaptations of pregnancy pathological?
cards.a: No — they are not pathological; rather they are highly coordinated responses.

cards.q: State the three purposes of the physiological adaptations of pregnancy.
cards.a: (1) To support the metabolic demands of the growing fetus and placenta; (2) to protect the mother from the physiological stressors of pregnancy and parturition, especially haemorrhage during delivery; and (3) to ensure adequate utero-placental perfusion for nutrient and gas exchange.

cards.q: When do the changes of pregnancy start, and what drives them?
cards.a: They start after conception, and are driven primarily by endocrine signals from the corpus luteum and the developing placenta — notably oestrogen, progesterone and human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG).

cards.q: What does the cardiovascular adaptation of pregnancy ensure?
cards.a: That oxygen and nutrients are delivered to the uterus and placenta.

cards.q: What two effects do elevated oestrogen and progesterone have on the cardiovascular system?
cards.a: They cause systemic vascular resistance to be lowered, and cardiac output to increase.

cards.q: By how much does plasma volume increase in pregnancy?
cards.a: By 40%–50% above the non-pregnant level.

cards.q: By how much does red blood cell mass increase, and what stimulates it?
cards.a: By only 15%–20%, stimulated by increased erythropoietin production.

cards.q: What is the physiological anaemia of pregnancy?
cards.a: Plasma volume expansion far outpaces the rise in RBC mass, leading to haemodilution — a fall in haemoglobin concentration and haematocrit.

cards.q: What is the clinical importance of the physiological anaemia of pregnancy?
cards.a: In pregnancy, haemoglobin levels of 10.5 g/dL to 11 g/dL are considered physiologically normal.

cards.q: By how much does cardiac output increase in pregnancy?
State the equation for cardiac output.
cards.a: Cardiac output (CO) = Heart Rate (HR) × Stroke Volume (SV).

cards.q: By how much does stroke volume increase, when, and why?
cards.a: By 20–30%, early in pregnancy, due to increased end-diastolic volume and myocardial hypertrophy.

cards.q: How does heart rate change in pregnancy?
cards.a: It increases progressively throughout pregnancy, by 15–20 beats per minute above baseline.

cards.q: By how much does systemic vascular resistance decrease?
State the three drivers of the fall in systemic vascular resistance.
cards.a: (i) The smooth muscle relaxing effect of progesterone; (ii) increased local production of vasodilators — nitric oxide (NO) and prostacyclin (PGI2); and (iii) the creation of a low-resistance, high-flow utero-placental circuit.

cards.q: Why does blood pressure fall in pregnancy despite the increase in cardiac output?
cards.a: Because of the massive drop in systemic vascular resistance.

cards.q: Which falls more in pregnancy, the systolic or the diastolic blood pressure?
cards.a: The diastolic drops more than the systolic.

cards.q: When is the lowest blood pressure reached in pregnancy, and what happens afterwards?
cards.a: Around 20–24 weeks, before gradually returning to the pre-pregnancy baseline at term.

cards.q: Why is 20 weeks gestation used as the cut-off for gestational hypertension (PIH)?
cards.a: Because the blood pressure physiologically falls to its lowest around 20–24 weeks before returning to baseline — so hypertension after 20 weeks can be attributed to the pregnancy.

cards.q: What happens when a pregnant woman lies supine?
cards.a: The gravid uterus compresses the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the lower aorta, leading to reduced venous return to the heart.

cards.q: What are the consequences of aortocaval compression?
cards.a: A drop in stroke volume and cardiac output, leading to supine hypotensive syndrome — dizziness, pallor and placental hypoperfusion.

cards.q: How should pregnant women in late pregnancy lie, and why?
cards.a: Always in left lateral tilt, to displace the uterus off the great vessels.

cards.q: What does the respiratory system adapt to ensure?
cards.a: That the increased metabolic demand for oxygen — elevated by roughly 20% — is met, and that fetal waste carbon dioxide is effectively cleared.

cards.q: By how much does tidal volume increase in pregnancy?
What happens to functional residual capacity in pregnancy?
cards.a: It is reduced by 10–20%.

cards.q: What is minute ventilation, and how does it change in pregnancy?
cards.a: Minute ventilation = tidal volume × respiratory rate; it increases by 30–40%.

cards.q: What happens to residual volume in pregnancy?
cards.a: It decreases by 15–20%.

cards.q: What happens to vital capacity in pregnancy?
cards.a: It is unchanged.

cards.q: What must the maternal kidneys process in pregnancy?
cards.a: Both maternal waste and fetal metabolic byproducts.

cards.q: What are the anatomical renal changes of pregnancy?
cards.a: Hydronephrosis and hydroureters.

cards.q: What are the two causes of hydronephrosis and hydroureter in pregnancy?
cards.a: (i) Progesterone-induced smooth muscle relaxation; and (ii) compression of the ureters at the pelvic brim by the expanding uterus.

cards.q: Why is hydronephrosis worse on the right than the left in pregnancy?
cards.a: Because of the dextrorotation of the uterus, and the cushioning effect on the left ureter by the sigmoid colon.

cards.q: What is the clinical significance of urinary stasis in pregnancy?
cards.a: It increases the risk of progression from asymptomatic bacteriuria to acute pyelonephritis.

cards.q: By how much does renal plasma flow increase in pregnancy?
cards.a: By up to 50%–80%.

cards.q: By how much does the glomerular filtration rate increase in pregnancy?
cards.a: By up to 50%.

cards.q: What does hyperfiltration lead to in pregnancy?
cards.a: Clearance of creatinine, urea and uric acid is highly elevated, leading to a decreased serum creatinine.

cards.q: What is the serum creatinine level in pregnancy, and what is the non-pregnant level?
cards.a: 0.4–0.6 mg/dL in pregnancy, against a non-pregnant level of 0.8 mg/dL.

cards.q: What does a creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL or higher in pregnancy indicate?
cards.a: It warrants evaluation for underlying renal impairment or pre-eclampsia.

cards.q: Why does glycosuria occur in pregnancy?
cards.a: Because the high GFR leads to exceeding the renal threshold for complete glucose reabsorption.

cards.q: What happens to urinary protein excretion in pregnancy?
cards.a: There is a mild increase.

cards.q: What is the nature of the GI changes of pregnancy, and what drives them?
cards.a: They are primarily functional, driven by the smooth muscle relaxant properties of progesterone.

cards.q: What effect does high progesterone have on the lower oesophageal sphincter, and what results?
cards.a: It decreases lower oesophageal sphincter (LES) tone, resulting in GERD (gastro-oesophageal reflux disease).

cards.q: What two factors combine to cause GERD in pregnancy?
cards.a: Decreased LES tone, combined with increased intra-abdominal pressure from the expanding uterus.

cards.q: What are the other effects of progesterone on the GI tract?
cards.a: Decreased intestinal motility; decreased gall bladder motility, leading to cholelithiasis; and constipation.

cards.q: What effect does oestrogen have on the gums in pregnancy?
cards.a: It causes gingival hyperplasia and bleeding gums — epulis gravidarum.

cards.q: What causes haemorrhoids in pregnancy?
cards.a: Constipation, and elevated venous pressure in the pelvic veins due to uterine compression of the vena cava.

cards.q: What happens to alkaline phosphatase in pregnancy, and why?
cards.a: It doubles or triples, due to placental production.

cards.q: How is the haematological state of pregnancy described, and why?
cards.a: As a hypercoagulable state — an evolutionary adaptation to prevent fatal postpartum haemorrhage, but one that carries significant risk of venous thromboembolism.

cards.q: Which procoagulant factors increase in pregnancy?
cards.a: Fibrinogen, VII, VIII, IX and XII, as well as von Willebrand factor.

cards.q: Which anticoagulant decreases in pregnancy, and what happens to fibrinolysis?
cards.a: Protein S decreases, and fibrinolysis is decreased.

cards.q: By how much is the risk of DVT and pulmonary embolism increased in pregnancy?
cards.a: Up to 4–5 fold.

cards.q: What happens to the white cell count in pregnancy?
cards.a: There is leucocytosis.

cards.q: What is the purpose of the endocrine changes of pregnancy?
cards.a: The endocrine milieu undergoes a complete revolution, transforming maternal metabolism to prioritise the continuous supply of glucose and amino acids to the fetus.

cards.q: Which hormones increase to produce insulin resistance in pregnancy?
cards.a: Oestrogen, human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol, and placental growth hormone.

cards.q: What must the maternal response be to hormone-induced insulin resistance?
cards.a: It must increase insulin secretion to maintain euglycaemia.

cards.q: When does gestational diabetes mellitus occur?
cards.a: When the maternal pancreatic response is insufficient to overcome the hormone-induced insulin resistance.

cards.q: By how much does the pituitary gland enlarge in pregnancy, and why?
cards.a: Up to 135%, due to primary hypertrophy and hyperplasia of lactotrophs (prolactin-producing cells), stimulated by oestrogen.

cards.q: What is the pituitary prone to in pregnancy, and under what circumstances?
cards.a: Sheehan syndrome, if severe postpartum haemorrhage and hypovolaemic shock occur.

cards.q: What causes hyperpigmentation in pregnancy?
cards.a: Elevated MSH (melanocyte stimulating hormone) and oestrogen.

cards.q: Which areas darken in pregnancy?
cards.a: The areolae, the perineum, the axillae, and the midline of the abdomen (linea nigra).

cards.q: Name the other skin changes of pregnancy.
cards.a: Melasma (chloasma); striae gravidarum; and vascular changes — palmar erythema and spider angiomas.

cards.q: What are the musculoskeletal changes of pregnancy?
cards.a: Progressive lumbar lordosis and joint laxity.
